name: Destructible Environment
description: Parts of the level can be damaged, fractured, removed, or converted into debris that changes navigation and tactics.
design_notes: Destructible environments affect more than visuals: chunk ids, damage thresholds, debris lifetime, navmesh rebuilds, save persistence, replication, and soft-lock prevention all need rules. The design should decide what destruction can solve, what it can never bypass, and how players read structural state before committing resources.
design_purpose: Let players reshape cover, paths, hazards, or combat sightlines through physical interaction.
Make powerful attacks or tools feel consequential in the world rather than only on health bars.
Create emergent tactical choices while preserving authored progression and performance budgets.
player_fantasy: The player feels that the environment reacts materially to their power and choices.

parameters.name: destructible_chunk_id
parameters.description: Stable chunk identifier used for damage accumulation, persistence, replication, and debug logs.

parameters.name: damage_impulse_threshold
parameters.description: Minimum impact or damage impulse required before the chunk changes fracture state.

parameters.name: fracture_graph_id
parameters.description: Data asset that defines how the chunk splits, collapses, or swaps to damaged geometry.

parameters.name: debris_lifetime_ms
parameters.description: Milliseconds debris remains interactive before fading, pooling, sleeping, or becoming static decoration.

parameters.name: navmesh_rebuild_policy_id
parameters.description: Policy for updating AI navigation after destruction changes walkable or blocked space.

edge_cases: Destroying a bridge, wall, or floor removes the only route to a required objective or spawn point.
A navmesh rebuild occurs while AI agents are pathing through the damaged area.
A late-joining client needs the exact destruction state without replaying every historical damage event.
Debris collides with newly fractured chunks and creates an escalating physics cost.
common_bugs: Destroyed chunk state is not saved, so reload restores blocked paths or duplicate rewards.
Damage is applied to child debris after fracture and recursively creates far more bodies than the performance budget allows.
Clients predict different fracture results because random chunk ordering or floating-point impulses are not deterministic.
AI cover and line-of-sight data still reference a wall that has been removed.
balancing_notes: Destruction is most satisfying when it opens options, but critical paths need fallback routes or repair rules.
Debris damage, cover removal, and visibility changes can swing combat difficulty more than the initial explosion.
Limit persistent destruction density per area if save size, navmesh rebuilds, or replication payloads become expensive.
accessibility_notes: Provide reduced debris, reduced camera shake, and reduced particle options for destruction-heavy scenes.
Use shape, silhouette, and UI cues to distinguish destructible surfaces from purely decorative damage.

implementation_notes.unity: Use stable destructible_chunk_id values and swap between intact, damaged, and fractured prefab states through one authority transaction. Batch Rigidbody debris activation, cap debris_lifetime_ms, rebuild NavMesh tiles asynchronously, and persist chunk state rather than relying on scene defaults.
implementation_notes.godot: Represent chunks as nodes with explicit fracture_state and optional debris scenes. Use NavigationServer region updates after destruction, pool debris bodies, and save chunk ids with state flags so streamed levels restore the same layout.
implementation_notes.unreal: Geometry Collections, Chaos destruction, or authored mesh swaps should be driven by server-approved damage events. Replicate chunk state or fracture commands deterministically, update navigation invokers or navmesh tiles, and move cosmetic debris to client-only when it no longer affects gameplay.
implementation_notes.web: Use authored tile or chunk swaps for most browser projects, reserving dynamic rigidbody debris for small counts. Keep destruction state in compact arrays keyed by chunk id, and debounce pathfinding or collision rebuilds after multiple nearby edits.

example_games.title: Red Faction: Guerrilla
example_games.note: Uses destructible structures as a major combat and sandbox feature.

example_games.title: Battlefield: Bad Company
example_games.note: Uses environmental destruction to change cover and sightlines.
